nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, plant-rich margins of lowland lakes in the lower Mamberamo River system; clear to slightly turbid warm water with abundant aquatic and marginal vegetation; foothill region surrounded by jungle

decorPreferences: dense marginal planting (Java fern, Vallisneria, Echinodorus)
open central swimming area at least 60 cm wide
dark substrate to intensify male coloration
floating plants or surface cover to diffuse lighting
driftwood or root tangles as retreat structure

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of prepared and live/frozen foods. Staple of high-quality small-pellet or flake food with spirulina component, supplemented with frozen or live daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms, and micro-pellets. Wild fish forage on zooplankton, insects, algae, and organic detritus. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily; varied diet supports intense male coloration.

avoidWith: very small fish or fry (may be predated)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by E. Prins, taken November 2000; specimen 8 cm TL. Copyright held by photographer; permission required before use.

summary: Bleher’s Rainbowfish is a strikingly coloured narrow endemic from Lake Holmes in West Papua, Indonesia. Males develop brilliant red-orange and blue-green iridescence at breeding display. Endangered (IUCN EN) due to its extremely restricted range; captive-bred stock is increasingly available in the hobby.
careNotes:
Bleher’s Rainbowfish (Chilatherina bleheri) was described by G. R. Allen in 1985 and named in honour of ichthyologist and explorer Heiko Bleher. It belongs to the genus Chilatherina, which is distinct from the true rainbowfishes of the larger genus Melanotaenia; both belong to the family Melanotaeniidae within the order Atheriniformes. The species is endemic to Lake Holmes (Danau Bira) in the lower Mamberamo River drainage of West Papua, Indonesia, with some reports extending to nearby Lake Tage; the extremely restricted range underpins its IUCN Endangered listing (assessed 2019, criteria B1ab(v)).

In the aquarium, a group of at least six (ideally more females than males, e.g., 2:4 or 3:6) should be maintained in a tank no shorter than 120 cm to allow the vigorous open-water swimming typical of rainbowfishes. Males develop spectacular red-orange flanks with blue-green iridescence on the dorsal surface when in display condition; a dark substrate and dense background planting intensify coloration. Water should be maintained on the slightly alkaline, moderately hard side (pH 7.0–8.0, gH 8–15, temperature 23–28°C) to reflect the neutral to slightly alkaline conditions of the Mamberamo lowland lake system. The species is an active, peaceful mid-water shoaler and combines well with other similarly-sized rainbowfishes, tetras, and calm catfish species. Due to its Endangered conservation status, hobbyists are strongly encouraged to source captive-bred specimens, which are increasingly available through specialist breeders and rainbowfish societies.
breedingNotes: Bleher’s Rainbowfish is an egg-scatterer that broadcasts adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops over several days. Conditioning adults on live and frozen foods (daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms) stimulates spawning behaviour. Eggs typically hatch in 7–10 days at 25–27°C. Fry are small and require infusoria or commercially prepared fry foods for the first 1–2 weeks before graduating to baby brine shrimp and micro-pellets. Removing eggs or spawning mops daily to a separate raising tank significantly improves fry survival, as adults will consume eggs given the opportunity. Captive breeding is considered moderately achievable and is actively practised by rainbowfish enthusiasts to reduce pressure on wild populations.

primary.name: FishBase — Chilatherina bleheri (species ID 25601)
primary.notes: Primary data source. Provided maximum size (12 cm SL), temperature range (23–27°C), pH (7.0), hardness (dH 8–15), native range (Lake Holmes, lower Mamberamo, Irian Jaya/Indonesia), taxonomy (Melanotaeniidae, Atheriniformes), IUCN Endangered status (assessed 2019, criteria B1ab(v)), trophic level 2.8 (omnivore), and medium population resilience. Temperature upper end extended to 28°C based on general Melanotaeniidae thermal tolerance consistent with Aquarium Co-Op rainbowfish care guide (24–28°C). pH upper bound set to 8.0 based on ecological context of Mamberamo lowland lake system.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rainbowfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Melanotaeniidae care guide. Provided tank size recommendation (minimum 4-ft/120 cm), temperature 24–28°C (75–82°F), pH 6–8, hardness 8–20 dGH, schooling requirements (groups of 6+, more females), diet preferences (variety of dry/frozen/live foods), and compatible tankmates. No Chilatherina bleheri-specific page exists; data used to contextualise and extend FishBase parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Chilatherina bleheri
additional.notes: Confirmed species etymology (named for Heiko Bleher), habitat description (shallow water with abundant plant life in foothill jungle region), and that three new rainbowfish species were described by Allen in 1985 from this area. Confirmed Lake Holmes distribution.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Chilatherina bleheri
additional.notes: Confirmed Endangered (EN) status with assessment date December 5, 2019, criteria B1ab(v). Species not evaluated under CITES or CMS. Restricted endemic range is the primary threat driver.

additional.name: FishBase — Chilatherina bleheri Photos (species ID 25601)
additional.notes: Two photos available by photographer E. Prins (Belgium). Image Chble_u9.jpg shows specimen at 8 cm TL taken November 2000. All rights reserved; permission required for reproduction.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; spawns over fine-leaved plants or mops. Condition with live/frozen foods. Remove eggs daily to raise separately; fry require infusoria then baby brine shrimp. Captive breeding increasingly practised to support conservation.
